20.prime1_title: Nobel Peace Prize goes to anti-nuclear group
20.prime1_text:
The Nobel Peace Prize has been awarded to the International Campaign for Nuclear Abolition for years of work to eliminate nuclear weapons and reduce the risk of a devastating war. The Norwegian Nobel Committee praised the group for bringing together grassroots activists, scientists, and survivors of nuclear testing. It said the organisation played a key role in building support for the Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons and helped shift the debate toward the humanitarian consequences of using them.

Speaking in Oslo, executive director Maria Santos described the award as a tribute to ordinary people who don’t want to live under the threat of nuclear war. She urged countries that possess nuclear weapons to join disarmament talks and invest more in health, climate, and education instead. Leaders in several countries welcomed the decision, while officials in nuclear-armed states insisted that deterrence is still essential to national security.

Analysts believe the prize could add diplomatic pressure ahead of next year’s non-proliferation review conference, where tensions between nuclear and non-nuclear states are likely to resurface. Founded in 2007, the campaign now includes more than 600 partner organisations worldwide. Much of its work has focused on survivor testimony and youth activism. Committee chair Anne Lunde said the award recognises years of steady work and moral leadership.
20.prime2_title: This Day in WMMO History - May 30th
20.prime2_text:
May 30 is a big date in WMMO’s history. Over the years, a lot has happened on this day, giving listeners and staff plenty to look back on.

In 1985, WMMO made the switch to a classic rock format. It was a bold move, especially in a competitive market, but it ended up shaping the station for decades. The first song that morning was The Rolling Stones’ “Start Me Up,” which set the tone for a new direction that quickly connected with listeners across Central Florida.

On May 30, 1999, WMMO kicked off its first Memorial Weekend Rock Marathon. The 48-hour event, with limited commercial breaks, paid tribute to some of rock’s biggest legends. It didn’t take long to become a fan favorite, helping boost ratings and further build the station’s reputation with serious music fans.

The date also marks a big step in 2012, when WMMO rolled out an updated mobile app. The launch pushed the station beyond traditional radio and into the hands of a growing digital audience. With backstage interviews and live concert streams, the app showed that the station was keeping up with changing technology rather than standing still.

From format flips to special broadcasts, May 30 says a lot about WMMO’s long-running commitment to classic rock—and to the loyal fans who still like to turn it up loud.

21.stimulus_title: Newly Discovered Prehistoric Worm Creature 'Strange Beyond Measure'
21.stimulus_text:
Scientists have discovered a prehistoric worm like creature which they describe as “strange beyond measure,” this finding shows an unusual branch of early animal evolution. Fossilized remains were found in 520 million year old marine sediments in China. The creatures revealed a soft bodied organism with a tubular form, ringed segments, and a circular mouth lined with sharp spines.

The creature measured just a few inches long, and it most likely lived on the seafloor during the Cambrian Period. Researchers believe the creature used its spiny mouth to latch onto prey or organic material, drawing food into a simple digestive tract and obtaining needed nutrients. The creature has an incredibly unique anatomy.

Lead paleontologist Dr. Li Wen said, “It’s strange beyond measure". “Its combination of features doesn’t fit into any known group, living or extinct.” Advanced imaging techniques revealed preserved muscle fibers and traces of internal organs, offering rare insight into soft-bodied animals that rarely are found as fossils.

The discovery suggests early marine ecosystems were more diverse than we originally thought. Scientists hope more study will clarify where this creature fits in evolution, and what it reveals about the past.

21.prime1_title: Why Wikipedia's cofounder wants to replace the online encyclopedia with the blockchain
21.prime1_text:
Jimmy Wales, the cofounder of Wikipedia, says the online encyclopedia he helped launch more than twenty years ago may need some meaningful updates. While speaking at a technology conference this week, Wales explained that Wikipedia’s long‑standing volunteer model has run into challenges in recent years, including a decline in active editors, internal disagreements, and increasing mistrust surrounding how content is moderated.

Under the idea he is proposing, articles and every edit made to them would be recorded on a public blockchain, creating a fully transparent history of contributions. People who make verified edits could earn digital tokens as a form of recognition, something Wales believes might motivate more subject‑matter experts to get involved. Smart contracts, essentially automated agreements written into code, could also be used to manage disputes and enforce editorial decisions based on guidelines approved by the community.

Wales argues that decentralizing the system in this way would limit the influence of small groups of administrators and make it more difficult for individuals to quietly manipulate entries. However, critics are not convinced. Some question whether blockchain technology could realistically handle the enormous traffic Wikipedia receives every day, while others warn that offering token rewards might encourage quantity over quality when it comes to edits.

The Wikimedia Foundation has responded by saying it is open to exploring emerging technologies, but it remains firmly committed to its nonprofit mission and the current community‑driven structure. Whether a blockchain‑based model could ever replace Wikipedia’s existing system is still uncertain, yet the ongoing debate highlights a broader feeling that the platform must continue to adapt as the internet evolves.
21.prime2_text:
At dawn, the marble halls of the Beijing Dance Academy come alive with the thud of pointe shoes and instructors calling out steady corrections. Founded in 1954, the school has grown into one of China’s leading institutions for ballet and classical dance, blending rigorous Russian-style training with elements of traditional Chinese artistry.

Students as young as eleven spend hours each day inside the academy’s studios. They balance regular academic classes with long, often exhausting rehearsals that test both body and mind. Many of the teachers are former principal dancers who emphasize discipline, precision, and emotional depth in every movement. Senior instructor Li Xinyue explains, “Technique is only the beginning. We teach our students to tell stories that connect with people everywhere.”

The studios feature the expected mirrors and barres, but they are also decorated with calligraphy scrolls and framed photographs from famous revolutionary ballets, a reminder of the school’s cultural roots. In recent years, graduates have gone on to perform with companies in Shanghai, London, and New York, demonstrating just how far the academy’s reputation now reaches.

Competition to enter the school is fierce. Thousands of hopefuls audition each year for only a handful of places, all dreaming of one day stepping onto major stages. As China places greater emphasis on promoting its culture, the academy has become more than a training ground; it stands as a symbol of that national effort. Administrators say new scholarships planned for next year are intended to open the doors to more students from rural areas and lower-income families, widening access to this demanding but prestigious path.
